title: Address Resolution Protocol
description: Resolves IPv4 addresses to MAC addresses within a local network segment.
fullExplanation: When a device wants to send data to an IP on the same subnet, it needs the destination MAC address. ARP broadcasts 'Who has 192.168.1.1? Tell 192.168.1.100' to all devices (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). The device with that IP replies with its MAC address. The result is cached in the ARP table for ~20 minutes (Windows) or ~1 minute (Linux). ARP only works within a single subnet — cross-subnet traffic goes to the default gateway.

process.description: Device A wants to reach 192.168.1.1 but doesn't know its MAC
Device A broadcasts ARP Request to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF
All devices on segment receive the broadcast
Device with IP 192.168.1.1 sends unicast ARP Reply with its MAC
Device A caches the IP→MAC mapping in its ARP table
Device A can now send Ethernet frames directly to that MAC

keyFacts: ARP cache timeout: ~20 min Windows, ~1 min Linux
Gratuitous ARP: device announces its own IP/MAC (used in HSRP/VRRP failover)
ARP has NO authentication — anyone can reply
Proxy ARP: router answers ARP on behalf of remote hosts
IPv6 uses NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) instead of ARP
ARP only works within a single broadcast domain (subnet)
